content:
# John Chapter 17 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 17 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 17:1 — "the hour has come" vs. "the time has come" and "glorify your Son that the Son may glorify you" vs. "Glorify your Son so he can give glory back to you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Father, the hour has come; glorify the Son of you, that the Son may glorify you" | Literal hOra; preserves "the Son of you" |
| LOGOS | "Father, the hour has come; glorify your Son that the Son may glorify you" | Same meaning; natural possessives |
| PNEUMA | "Father, the time has come. Glorify your Son so he can give glory back to you" | "Time" for hOra; "give glory back" reinterprets mutual glorification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Hour" (hOra) is a key Johannine term — distinct from kairos or chronos — and must be preserved. PNEUMA's "give glory back" implies return of something borrowed, which misrepresents the mutual glorification.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "time" and "give glory back" each introduce interpretive shifts.

---

## 17:2 — "authority over all flesh" vs. "authority over everyone"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you gave him authority over all flesh" | Literal pasEs sarkos |
| LOGOS | "you have given him authority over all flesh" | Same; present perfect |
| PNEUMA | "you have given him authority over everyone" | "Everyone" replaces "all flesh" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "all flesh." The phrase pasa sarx is a Hebraism (kol-basar) meaning "all humanity" — its Old Testament resonance should be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "everyone" loses the Hebraism and its OT connections.

---

## 17:3 — "that they know you" vs. "this is the way to have eternal life — to know you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "this is the eternal life, that they may know you, the only true God, and [the one] whom you sent, Yeshua Christos" | Literal hina clause; article with "eternal life" |
| LOGOS | "this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent" | Natural English; indicative "know" |
| PNEUMA | "this is the way to have eternal life — to know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, the one you sent to earth" | "The way to have" — shifts from definition to method; adds "to earth" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The verse defines what eternal life IS, not how to acquire it. "This is eternal life, that they know you" = eternal life consists in knowing God. PNEUMA's "the way to have" makes it a pathway rather than a definition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Also, PNEUMA adds "to earth" — not in the Greek.

---

## 17:5 — "in your own presence" vs. "with yourself" vs. "bring me into the glory we shared"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "glorify me, you Father, with yourself, with the glory that I had with you before the world was" | Literal para seautO; "before the world was" |
| LOGOS | "glorify me in your own presence with the glory that I had with you before the world existed" | "In your own presence" for para seautO |
| PNEUMA | "bring me into the glory we shared before the world began" | "Bring me into" replaces "glorify me with"; "we shared" simplifies |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "In your own presence" captures para seautO (literally "alongside/beside yourself") and conveys the intimacy of the request. PNEUMA's "bring me into" loses the "glorify" verb (doxason).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 17:6 — "the people" vs. "the men" vs. "the ones"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the men whom you gave me from the world" | Literal tois anthrOpois |
| LOGOS | "the people whom you gave me out of the world" | "People" — gender-inclusive rendering |
| PNEUMA | "the ones you gave me from this world" | "The ones" — neutral; "this world" adds demonstrative |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "people." AnthrOpois here means "humans/people" — not exclusively male. "People" is gender-inclusive and accurate.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 17:8 — "words" vs. "message" for rEmata

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the words that you gave me I have given to them" | Literal ta rEmata |
| LOGOS | "the words that you gave me" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "I have passed on to them the message you gave me" | "Message" replaces "words"; "passed on" for "given" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "words." REma (plural rEmata) means specific spoken words — "message" (PNEUMA) is more abstract. The plural "words" preserves the concrete sense.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 17:11 — "keep them in your name" vs. "protect them by the power of your name"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "keep them in the name of you that you have given me" | Literal tErEson autous en tO onomati sou |
| LOGOS | "keep them in your name, which you have given me" | Same; "which" for relative pronoun |
| PNEUMA | "you have given me your name; now protect them by the power of your name so that they will be united just as we are" | "Protect by the power of" interprets "keep in"; splits the sentence; adds "power" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Keep them in your name" means to guard them within the sphere of the divine name/identity. PNEUMA's "protect them by the power of your name" shifts from location/sphere ("in") to instrument ("by the power of") — a different theological emphasis.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The en (in) is locative/spherical, not instrumental.

---

## 17:12 — "the son of destruction" vs. "the one headed for destruction"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the son of the destruction" | Literal ho huios tEs apOleias — Semitic idiom |
| LOGOS | "the son of destruction" | Same without articles |
| PNEUMA | "the one headed for destruction" | "Headed for" interprets the Semitic idiom |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "the son of destruction." This is a Semitic idiom (ben + abstract noun = one characterized by that quality) and should be preserved as a recognizable biblical phrase (cf. 2 Thess 2:3).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "headed for" is a valid interpretation but loses the idiom.

---

## 17:13 — "my joy fulfilled in themselves" vs. "filled with my joy"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the joy of me made full in themselves" | Literal; tEn charan tEn emEn; passive plEroO |
| LOGOS | "my joy fulfilled in themselves" | Same meaning; "fulfilled" |
| PNEUMA | "they would be filled with my joy" | Active voice; "filled with" changes syntax |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "My joy fulfilled in themselves" preserves the reflexive — the joy is fulfilled within them, not merely poured into them.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 17:15 — "the evil one" vs. "the evil one" vs. "safe from the evil one"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "keep them from the evil [one]" | tou ponErou — ambiguous: "evil" or "evil one" |
| LOGOS | "keep them from the evil one" | Personal interpretation of tou ponErou |
| PNEUMA | "keep them safe from the evil one" | Adds "safe" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "The evil one" is the correct interpretation in Johannine context (cf. 1 John 2:13-14, 3:12, 5:18-19). PNEUMA's "safe" adds a word not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 17:17 — "Sanctify them in the truth" vs. "Make them holy by your truth; teach them your word, which is truth"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Sanctify them in the truth; the word of you is truth" | Literal hagiason; en tE alEtheia |
| LOGOS | "Sanctify them in the truth; your word is truth" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Make them holy by your truth; teach them your word, which is truth" | "Make them holy" for hagiason; adds "teach them" — not in Greek; "by" for en |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Sanctify" is the established theological term for hagiason and should be retained. PNEUMA adds "teach them" which is not in the verse. The "in" (en) should be preserved — sanctification happens within the sphere of truth, not merely by means of it.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 17:19 — "I consecrate myself" vs. "I sanctify myself" vs. "I give myself as a holy sacrifice"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for them I sanctify myself" | Same verb hagiazO as v.17 |
| LOGOS | "for their sake I consecrate myself" | "Consecrate" — priestly nuance of hagiazO when self-directed |
| PNEUMA | "I give myself as a holy sacrifice for them so they can be made holy by your truth" | "Holy sacrifice" — extensive interpretive expansion; "made holy" for hagiasmenoi |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "consecrate." When Jesus applies hagiazO to himself, the priestly/sacrificial sense is paramount — he sets himself apart as the offering. "Consecrate" captures this priestly nuance better than "sanctify." PNEUMA's "holy sacrifice" is interpretively defensible but adds a noun not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The distinction between "sanctify" (v.17, for disciples) and "consecrate" (v.19, self-referential) is a deliberate LOGOS choice that SOPHIA affirms.

---

## 17:23 — "become perfectly one" vs. "be perfected into one" vs. "experience such perfect unity"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that they may be perfected into one" | Literal teteiOmenoi eis hen; perfect passive participle |
| LOGOS | "that they may become perfectly one" | Adverb "perfectly" modifying "one" |
| PNEUMA | "May they experience such perfect unity that the world will know" | "Experience" replaces "become"; "unity" is abstract noun |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "become perfectly one." TeteiOmenoi (from teleioO, "to perfect/complete") modifies the process of becoming one — the unity is perfected/brought to completion. PNEUMA's "experience" is too passive and subjective.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 17:24 — "I desire" vs. "I wish" vs. "I want"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I wish that where I am, they also may be with me" | thelO = "wish" |
| LOGOS | "I desire that they also... may be with me where I am" | thelO = "desire" — stronger |
| PNEUMA | "I want these whom you have given me to be with me where I am" | thelO = "want" — most direct |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "I desire." ThelO in this context carries the weight of deep purposeful will/desire — stronger than casual "want" (PNEUMA) and more intentional than "wish" (EMET).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. This is the only verse where Jesus expresses thelO in the prayer — the word choice matters.

---

## 17:25 — "O righteous Father" vs. "Righteous Father"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Righteous Father" | Literal pater dikaie without vocative particle |
| LOGOS | "O righteous Father" | Adds "O" — English vocative marker |
| PNEUMA | "O righteous Father" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "O righteous Father." The English vocative "O" is appropriate for formal prayer address.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. Minor stylistic choice.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adding words not in Greek | 6 | "to earth" (v.3), "power" (v.11), "safe" (v.15), "teach them" (v.17), "holy sacrifice" (v.19), "back" (v.1) |
| PNEUMA reinterpreting theological definitions | 2 | "Way to have eternal life" vs. definition (v.3), "experience unity" vs. "become one" (v.23) |
| Key term divergence | 4 | hOra: hour/time (v.1), sarx: flesh/everyone (v.2), hagiazO: sanctify/consecrate/make holy (vv.17, 19), thelO: desire/wish/want (v.24) |
| PNEUMA changing prepositions | 2 | "In your name" → "by the power of" (v.11), "in the truth" → "by your truth" (v.17) |
| EMET inverted/awkward syntax | 3 | vv.1, 6, 24 — possessive constructions and word order |
| LOGOS and SOPHIA near-identical | ~22 | Most verses identical |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

John 17 — the High Priestly Prayer — is the most theologically concentrated chapter in the Farewell Discourse, and the disagreements reflect this density. The most consequential divergence is in v.3, where PNEUMA's "the way to have eternal life" transforms John's ontological definition (eternal life IS knowing God) into a methodological statement (this is HOW you get eternal life). This is not merely a stylistic difference but a theological one.

The hagiazO/sanctify/consecrate question (vv.17, 19) reveals a sophisticated translation choice by LOGOS, which SOPHIA affirms: using "sanctify" for the disciples (v.17) but "consecrate" for Jesus' self-offering (v.19) captures the different nuances of the same Greek verb in its two contexts.

PNEUMA's consistent shift from "in" (locative/spherical) to "by" (instrumental) in phrases like "in your name" and "in the truth" represents a broader theological tendency to view divine attributes as instruments rather than environments. SOPHIA and LOGOS preserve the locative sense, which implies dwelling within the sphere of God's name/truth rather than merely using them as tools.

The chapter's prayer language is deeply personal and liturgical — LOGOS captures this register most faithfully, while PNEUMA's conversational tone occasionally domesticates the solemnity of Jesus' final prayer.

---
*Generated by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA — 2026-03-05*
